anchor.snippet: a matrix ( pl. : matrices ) is a rectangular array of numbers or other mathematical objects
denotes a matrix with two rows and three columns
Square matrices , matrices with the same number of rows and columns
a square matrix is invertible if and only if it has a nonzero determinant
A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers (or other mathematical objects), called the "entries"
A real matrix and a complex matrix are matrices whose entries are respectively real numbers or complex numbers
The horizontal and vertical lines of entries in a matrix are respectively called rows and columns
The size of a matrix is defined by the number of rows and columns it contains
A matrix with m rows and n columns is called an m × n matrix
Matrices with a single row are called row matrices or row vectors
A matrix with the same number of rows and columns is called a square matrix
A matrix with an infinite number of rows or columns (or both) is called an infinite matrix
it is useful to consider a matrix with no rows or no columns, called an empty matrix
Matrix addition and subtraction require matrices of a consistent size, and are calculated entrywise
addition is commutative , that is, the matrix sum does not depend on the order of the summands
The product c A of a number c (also called a scalar in this context) and a matrix A is computed by multiplying each entry of A by c
The transpose of an m × n matrix A is the n × m matrix A T (also denoted A tr or t A ) formed by turning rows into columns and vice versa
The transpose is compatible with addition and scalar multiplication
If A is an m × n matrix and B is an n × p matrix, then their matrix product AB is the m × p matrix

annotations.label: Associativity and distributivity of matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: Matrix multiplication satisfies the rules
matrix multiplication is not commutative , in marked contrast to (rational, real, or complex) numbers
There are three types of row operations
A submatrix of a matrix is a matrix obtained by deleting any collection of rows or columns or both
A principal submatrix is a square submatrix obtained by removing certain rows and columns
Matrices can be used to compactly write and work with multiple linear equations
If n = m and the equations are independent , then this can be done by writing
A real m -by- n matrix A gives rise to a linear transformation
the 2 × 2 matrix [MATH] can be viewed as the transform of the unit square into a parallelogram
Under the 1-to-1 correspondence between matrices and linear maps, matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of maps
The rank of a matrix A is the maximum number of linearly independent row vectors of the matrix
The rank–nullity theorem states that the dimension of the kernel of a matrix plus the rank equals the number of columns
A square matrix is a matrix with the same number of rows and columns
Square matrices of a given dimension form a noncommutative ring
If all entries of A below the main diagonal are zero, A is called an upper triangular matrix
The identity matrix I n of size n is the n -by- n matrix in which all the elements on the main diagonal are equal to 1
A scalar multiple of an identity matrix is called a scalar matrix
A square matrix A that is equal to its transpose, that is, A = A T , is a symmetric matrix
symmetry is often replaced by the concept of Hermitian matrices , which satisfies A ∗ = A
By the spectral theorem , real symmetric matrices and complex Hermitian matrices have an eigenbasis
A square matrix A is called invertible or non-singular if there exists a matrix B such that
If B exists, it is unique and is called the inverse matrix of A
A symmetric real matrix A is called positive-definite if the associated quadratic form
A symmetric matrix is positive-definite if and only if all its eigenvalues are positive
Allowing as input two different vectors instead yields the bilinear form associated to A
An orthogonal matrix is a square matrix with real entries whose columns and rows are orthogonal unit vectors
An orthogonal matrix A is necessarily invertible
A special orthogonal matrix is an orthogonal matrix with determinant +1
The trace , tr( A ) of a square matrix A is the sum of its diagonal entries

annotations.label: Trace of product independent of order

anchor.snippet: the trace of the product of two matrices is independent of the order of the factors
the trace of a matrix is equal to that of its transpose
The determinant of a square matrix A (denoted det( A ) or | A | ) is a number encoding certain properties of the matrix
A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero
The determinant of 2 × 2 matrices is given by
The determinant of a product of square matrices equals the product of their determinants
the Laplace expansion expresses the determinant in terms of minors
Determinants can be used to solve linear systems using Cramer's rule

An important case concerns sparse matrices , that is, matrices whose entries are mostly zero
The LU decomposition factors matrices as a product of lower ( L ) and an upper triangular matrices ( U )
Singular value decomposition (SVD) expresses any matrix A as a product UDV ∗
The eigendecomposition or diagonalization expresses A as a product VDV −1
the Jordan decomposition transforms a matrix into Jordan normal form

the category of matrices with entries in a field [MATH] with multiplication as composition is equivalent to the category of finite-dimensional vector spaces
